title: Equilibrium Stabilization of a Hidden Phase Like Metallic State in 1T-TaS2
authors: Turgut Yilmaz, Anil Rajapitamahuni, Suji Park, Houk Jang, Asish K. Kundu, Elio Vescovo
abstract: Electronic phases that lie outside the equilibrium ground state offer a route to explore competing configurations in correlated materials. In 1T-TaS2, ultrafast excitation accesses a metallic hidden phase that is distinct from the commensurate insulating ground state. Here we use angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy to show that an equivalent electronic configuration is stabilized in exfoliated intermediate-thickness 1T-TaS2 flakes, where it persists up to room temperature before evolving through a different sequence of electronic transitions. This equilibrium hidden-phase-like state hosts a metallic band with finite Fermi-level spectral weight while retaining the characteristic hybridization gaps associated with the star-of-David band folding. These results establish a platform for controlling competing electronic states in layered materials, with implications for both quantum science and phase change technologies.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the transient 'hidden' metallic phase of 1T-TaS2 can be stabilized as a stable equilibrium state by controlling the thickness of exfoliated flakes. This discovery provides a way to access and control competing electronic configurations in correlated materials without needing ultrafast laser excitation. The findings have significant implications for developing new phase-change technologies and quantum electronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to understand how to stabilize the metastable 'hidden' metallic phase of 1T-TaS2 in equilibrium and how electronic phases evolve with material thickness and temperature.
main_result: The researchers discovered that intermediate-thickness 1T-TaS2 flakes spontaneously host a stable, equilibrium metallic state (HPLMS) that mimics the transient hidden phase seen in bulk crystals under excitation.
method: High-resolution angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) was performed on mechanically exfoliated 1T-TaS2 flakes of varying thicknesses using the mu-ARPES technique.
model_or_system: The physical system is 1T-TaS2 (tantalum disulfide), a layered transition metal dichalcogenide known for its complex charge density wave (CDW) and Mott insulating phases.
key_observables: Spectral weight at the Fermi level, metallic band (MB) intensity, hybridization gaps, Fermi surface maps, and energy-momentum (E-k) dispersions.
important_parameters: Material thickness (ranging from 2.5 nm to 55 nm), temperature (50 K to 380 K), and out-of-plane momentum (kz).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the free-electron final-state approximation for determining out-of-plane momentum.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows dimensional control and thickness-dependent spectra; Figure 2 illustrates kz periodicity and Fermi surface maps in 25 nm flakes; Figure 3 details temperature-dependent EDCs and MDCs; Figure 4 presents a schematic phase diagram as a function of thickness and temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of metastable phases, describes the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents thickness-dependent spectroscopic results, analyzes temperature-driven phase evolution, and concludes with a proposed phase diagram.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Dimensional control of the electronic structure in 1T-TaS2. ARPES spectra along Γ–M are shown for bulk and exfoliated ﬂakes with thicknesses of 55, 24, 8, and 2.5 nm. Bulk crystals exhibit the C-CDW state with a lower Hubbard band at ≈∼200 meV marked with red parabola with no spectral weight at EF. By contrast, 24 and 55 nm ﬂakes display a shallow Γ-centered MB crossing EF marked with yellow dashed parabolas, consistent with stabilization of the HPLMS under equilibrium conditions. Thinner ﬂakes show progressively degraded spectra: the 8 nm sample retains a broadened MB, while the 2.5 nm ﬂake evolves into a fully gapped state with incoherent features marked with dashed black line....

Figure 2: kz periodicity and photon-energy dependence of the MB in exfoliated 25 nm thick 1T-TaS2. a, Fermi surface map in the (k∥, kz) plane showing strong intensity of the MB only at selected kz values. b, Corresponding E–kz dispersion highlighting the Γ-centered MB. c, Fermi surface maps at hν = 92 eV (kz ≈Γ) and 120 eV (kz ≈A), showing a bright pocket at Γ that vanishes at A. d, E–k∥dispersions at the same photon energies, reinforcing the photon-energy dependent modulation of MB. All ARPES data is recorded at 50 K sample temperature.

Figure 3: Temperature-dependent ARPES of a 24 nm 1T-TaS2 ﬂake. a, ARPES spectra along Γ–M recorded between 50 and 380 K. At low T, the MB coexists with a CDW-related suppression near 0.2–0.3 eV below EF , persisting up to 300 K. Above 300 K, the suppression weakens, and at 370–380 K the CDW feature collapses, leaving only a broadened MB. b, Energy distribution curves (EDCs) at Γ showing a sharp loss of MB spectral weight across 370 K. c, Momentum distribution curves (MDCs) at EF , integrated within 30 meV below EF, evolving from a broad MB-dominated proﬁle at low T to distinct side-band peaks at high T. d, EDCs at k∥= 0.36 ˚A−1, where the CDW-related peak at ∼0.23 eV (blue markers) is...

Figure 4: Schematic phase diagram of 1T-TaS2 as a function of thickness and tem- perature. In bulk crystals, the well-known sequence of charge-density-wave (CDW) phases is recovered: commensurate (C-CDW, Mott insulating), nearly commensurate (NC), and incom- mensurate (IC), with the C-CDW transition occurring at ∼180 K on cooling and ∼240 K on warming. Nanometer-thick ﬂakes bypass the bulk-like 240 K transition and instead stabilize a HPLMS that persists up to room temperature before evolving into a mixed HP+IC regime and ﬁnally the IC phase above ∼370 K. In the few-layer limit, the system becomes a band insulator with no clear CDW order. Band sketches illustrate representative electronic...
